retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Lymphatic drainage of the anterior two-thirds of the tongue is to the submental and submandibular lymph nodes, emptying into the deep cervical chain of lymph nodes. The posterior third of the tongue drains directly into the deep cervical lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The lymphatic vessels from the body of the tongue are divided into marginal and central. The marginal vessels drain the tongue's apex, marginal surface, and sublingual region into the submental and submandibular nodes. [10] Less often, they drain into the jugulodigastric or jugulo-omohyoid nodes. The central vessels are located at the tip of the tongue, travel through the mylohyoid muscle, and drain into the jugulo-omohyoid or jugulodigastric nodes. [10] The dorsal vessels collect lymphatic drainage from the area posterior to the sulcus and drain into the jugulo-omohyoid and jugulodigastric nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Arterial Supply and Lymphatic Drainage of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: The arterial supply to the tongue derives from branches of the external carotid artery, primarily through the lingual artery and the tonsillar branch of the facial artery. Venous drainage occurs through tributaries to the lingual vein. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Arterial Supply and Lymphatic Drainage of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: Lymphatic drainage of the oral tongue primarily leads to levels 1 through 3 of the neck, which includes the submental (level 1a) and submandibular (level 1b) lymph node basins, as well as the upper jugular chain lymphatics (levels 2 and 3). Conversely, lymphatic drainage from the tongue base primarily flows to levels 2 through 4. [9] [10] Understanding the lymphatic system is crucial for the locoregional management of tongue cancers. Neck dissection should be considered in the treatment of squamous cell carcinoma of the tongue due to the risk of cervical lymph node metastases, even in clinically negative necks, which can harbor hidden metastases at a rate of 20%. [11] Additionally, tumor thickness is correlated with cervical lymph node metastases. [12] [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Glossectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Arterial Supply and Lymphatic Drainage of the Tongue
retrieved_docs.content: As a result, the burden of nodal disease is a predictor of increased mortality. Elective neck dissection has been shown to provide a survival advantage and higher disease-free survival rates compared to therapeutic neck dissection. [15] [16] Occasionally, lower lymph nodes in levels 3 and 4 can harbor metastases even when levels 1 or 2 do not show disease, indicating a need for more aggressive management. [17] Therefore, neck dissection is strongly recommended as it offers a more accurate evaluation of lymph node status.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lymph Nodes -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Drainage Level VIb drains the efferent lymphatics from the anterior floor of the mouth, tip of the oral tongue, lower lip, thyroid gland, glottic and supraglottic larynx, hypopharynx, and cervical esophagus.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Swallowing -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The submental group of nodes primarily drain the floor of the oral cavity and the anterior part of the tongue. The submandibular group drains both the hard and soft palates, the mucosa of both cheeks, and contributes to drainage of the mouth and anterior tongue. The upper jugular cluster of nodes drains the parotid gland and receives secondary drainage from the submental and submandibular node clusters, as well as the pharynx and larynx. The middle jugular cluster of nodes directly drains the base of the tongue, hypopharynx, as well as receiving drainage from the upper jugular cluster. The lower jugular group of nodes primarily drains efferent lymphatics from the upper and middle jugular nodes as well as drainage from the hypopharynx. The medial supraclavicular cluster of nodes receives drainage from the lower jugular nodes, as well as the hypopharynx and esophagus. The posterior triangle group of nodes drains the nasopharynx and oropharynx. The anterior compartment of nodes drains the anterior floor of the mouth, the anterior-most part of the tongue, hypopharynx, and cervical esophagus. The retropharyngeal nodes drain the nasopharynx and the soft palate, while the retrostyloid nodes drain just efferent lymph via the nasopharynx. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Blood supply to the tongue comes predominantly from the lingual artery , a branch of the external carotid artery. [8] Venous drainage is done by the lingual vein .

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The suprahyoid artery supplies the omohyoid, sternothyroid, and thyrohyoid muscles. They anastomose with the corresponding vessels from the opposite side.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The dorsal lingual artery arises beneath the hyoglossus muscle and passes to the posterior part of the dorsum of the tongue. It supplies the mucous membrane of this region as well as the glossopalatine arch, lingual tonsils, soft palate, and epiglottis. [9] They anastomose with their corresponding vessels on the opposite side.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Tongue -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The deep lingual artery , which is the termination of the lingual artery, passes between the genioglossus muscle and inferior longitudinal muscle, supplying the apex of the tongue. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Palatoglossus Muscle (Glossopalatinus, Palatoglossal) -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The arterial supply is from the lingual artery, which is the branch of the external carotid artery. Some collateral circulation also comes from the branch of the facial artery (tonsillar artery). The lymphatic drainage is into the deep cervical group of lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Anterior Cervical Region -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Lymphatic Drainage of the Anterior Cervical Region
retrieved_docs.content: Facial lymphatic drainage in the area includes the submandibular nodes, which are deep to the submandibular gland within the submandibular triangle and drain the oral cavity and lower face soft tissue structures. Another important lymphatic drainage basin of the anterior cervical neck is the submental lymph nodes, which lie in the submental triangle and drain the floor of the mouth, tongue apex, and lower lip. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Styloglossus -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The styloglossus muscle receives its blood supply from the sublingual branch of the lingual artery. The lingual artery is a direct branch of the external carotid artery; it takes off from the external carotid at the level of the greater horn of the hyoid bone [1] . After this, the lingual artery travels between the hyoglossus (superficial) and middle pharyngeal constrictor (deep) to supply blood to the tongue. Dorsal lingual arteries supply the base of the tongue, whereas deep lingual arteries supply the body of the tongue. [7] Venous drainage of the tongue is via deep lingual veins and a single dorsal lingual vein; these vessels drain into the sublingual vein, which in turn empties directly into the internal jugular vein.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lymph Nodes -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Level III: Middle Jugular Group
retrieved_docs.content: Drainage This group drains the efferent lymphatics from levels II and V and partially from the retropharyngeal, paratracheal, and recurrent laryngeal nodes. It receives direct drainage from the base of the tongue, tonsils, larynx, hypopharynx, and thyroid gland.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Artery -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Generally, the length of the lingual artery is 75% of the length of the tongue, from the dorsal portion up to the tip. The path of the artery has a lateral and inferior direction for about three-quarters of its length.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2787
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that serves as the parent concept for Clinical actions A – G and is identified as the answer to the Chinese‑language diagnostic phrase “结直肠癌主要诊断的程序:发现:时间点:^患者:名义型:PhenX,” linking it to colorectal cancer diagnostic processes. Within the Excision hierarchy, a wide array of specific operative methods are enumerated, including Laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord and/or cauda equina (excluding procedures that involve more than two cervical vertebral segments), several variants of cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct (with or without cholangiography, transduodenal sphincterotomy, or sphincteroplasty), Choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage or calculus removal, cranial procedures such as infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy, laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration, nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and multiple forms of parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration (including approaches with mediastinal exploration, sternal split, or transthoracic access). The category also designates “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx” as a method of Excision and records a relationship to plastic procedures on the nose, indicating its relevance to both therapeutic and reconstructive interventions in the facial region. This integrated view underscores Excision as a versatile procedural framework encompassing diverse operative techniques, specific exclusions, and connections to both diagnostic pathways and plastic surgical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 619
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that encompasses a variety of operative methods. It includes arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from a joint, several types of cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including choledochoenterostomy and transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty with or without cholangiography), exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial, laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and disc excision at a single lumbar interspace, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration combined with mediastinal approaches such as sternal split, transthoracic access, or cervical mediastinal exploration. Excision also serves as the methodological basis for other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, as well as for percutaneous nephrolithotomy (perkutánní nefrolitotomie). In addition, Excision is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, indicating its relevance to reconstructive nasal surgery. Certain extensive spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category, namely laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy when more than two vertebral segments are involved in either the cervical or lumbar spine. The hierarchical structure places Clinical actions A‑G as a parent group under Excision, and Excision is also referenced in a colorectal‑cancer diagnostic protocol, highlighting its role in broader clinical pathways. Overall, Excision functions as a central concept that unites diverse operative techniques while delineating specific exclusions and connections to related procedural domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
retrieved_docs.content: Excision serves as a broad parent category for a variety of surgical actions, including the set of procedures labeled “Clinical actions A‑G.” Within this overarching category, many specific operative methods are classified as forms of excision: cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (both open and laparoscopic), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) that decompress nerve roots and may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. At the same time, the excision category explicitly excludes several laminectomy techniques used for spinal stenosis: thoracic laminectomy with cord or cauda equina decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two vertebral segments; and cervical, lumbar, or thoracic laminectomy for more than two vertebral segments performed under the same constraints. Excision is also linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional techniques extend beyond deep organ surgery to include facial and upper aerodigestive tract interventions. This integrated view highlights excision as a versatile surgical concept that encompasses numerous specific operative methods while delineating clear boundaries for procedures it does not cover.

Excision is also linked to perkutánní nefrolitotomie (percutaneous nephrolithotomy) and is noted to have a

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 624
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that encompasses a variety of operative techniques across multiple organ systems. Specific procedures that are classified as methods of Excision include partial or complete adrenalectomy or adrenal gland exploration (performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, with or without biopsy), arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from a joint, cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with optional cholangiography), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and several forms of parathyroid surgery such as standard parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a cervical approach, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via a transthoracic approach. Additional therapeutic applications of Excision extend to other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, as well as percutaneous nephrolithotomy. Conversely, certain spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. These excluded methods comprise laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina—without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—performed for spinal stenosis across various spinal regions (sacral, thoracic, cervical, and lumbar) and differing numbers of vertebral segments (one or two segments versus more than two segments). Excision is also linked, albeit in a non‑procedural context, as the answer to a specific colorectal‑cancer diagnostic program entry, and it holds a relationship to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, these connections illustrate that Excision functions as an overarching term for many targeted surgical removals while clearly distinguishing itself from certain decompressive spinal surgeries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1879
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of operative techniques and is positioned within the larger group of Clinical actions A‑G. It encompasses arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from joints, multiple forms of cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy, and cholangiography), and cranial procedures such as exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region and suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression. Excision also covers orbital exploration by a transcranial approach, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, foraminotomy, partial facetectomy, and disc excision, as well as nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision. Parathyroid surgery falls under this umbrella, including standard parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, and combined mediastinal‑exploration procedures. In addition to these methods, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and maintains a specific relationship with plastic procedures on the nose. Certain extensive spinal procedures—such as multisegment thoracic laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. Together, these relationships define Excision as a versatile operative concept that integrates numerous specialized techniques while delineating its scope relative to broader clinical actions and excluded methods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2167
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent group “Clinical actions A‑G.” It includes a wide range of operative methods such as partial or complete adrenalectomy (with or without biopsy) performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, both with and without cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, optionally combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; transcranial exploration of the orbit with lesion removal; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and disc excision in a single lumbar interspace; laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration; radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration; and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical or transthoracic approaches. The Excision category explicitly excludes certain laminectomy procedures: lumbar laminectomy with exploration and decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina that involves more than two vertebral segments, and cervical laminectomy with similar exploration that does not include facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. In addition to these operative techniques, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is related to plastic procedures on the nose. This integrated framework illustrates how Excision groups diverse anatomical surgeries while defining specific procedural exclusions and connections to related therapeutic domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1572
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is identified as the response to the colorectal‑cancer diagnostic program “结直肠癌主要诊断的程序:发现:时间点:^患者:名义型:PhenX,” indicating its role in the work‑up of colorectal malignancy. In the context of surgical classification, Excision explicitly excludes several laminectomy procedures that involve exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—specifically the cervical, lumbar (except for spondylolisthesis), sacral, and thoracic 1‑ or 2‑segment laminectomies. Conversely, Excision encompasses a broad range of operative methods, including Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct (procedure), Cholecystectomy with exploration of common duct with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty (with or without cholangiography), Choledochotomy or choledochostomy with exploration, drainage, or removal of calculus (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), Craniectomy or craniotomy, exploratory; infratentorial (posterior fossa), Laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with decompression of nerve root(s), including partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and/or excision of herniated intervertebral disc, re‑exploration, single interspace (cervical), Nipple exploration with excision of lactiferous duct, Orchiectomy, radical, for tumor with abdominal exploration, Parathyroidectomy or exploration of parathyroid(s), and Parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration by transthoracic approach. Additionally, Excision is described as a “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it is linked to “plastic procedures on nose.” Together, these relationships portray Excision as a versatile procedural concept that is applied to specific therapeutic surgeries, excluded from certain spinal laminectomies, and connected to both diagnostic pathways for colorectal cancer and reconstructive or therapeutic interventions of the head and neck region.

Importantly, the Excision class explicitly excludes pure laminectomy‑type spinal decompressions that lack facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision; these excluded methods cover cervical, lumbar, sacral, and thoracic laminectomies performed on one or two levels as well as multi‑segment laminectomies. This distinction clarifies that such isolated

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1707
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a surgical approach that serves multiple diagnostic and therapeutic roles across a wide range of anatomical sites. It is identified as the answer to the colorectal‑cancer diagnostic item “结直肠癌主要诊断的程序:发现:时间点:^患者:名义型:PhenX,” indicating that tissue removal may be a key step in confirming the disease. In neurosurgery, Excision is the method employed in both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy procedures. It also encompasses the transcranial exploration of the orbit with lesion removal, nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, and radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration. Excision is the designated technique for parathyroid surgery, including standard parathyroidectomy or exploration of the parathyroid glands, as well as more extensive approaches that combine mediastinal exploration via cervical, sternal‑split, or transthoracic routes. In addition, Excision functions as a method for “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” and it is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, reflecting its relevance in otolaryngologic and cosmetic surgery. Conversely, certain spinal decompression operations are explicitly excluded from the definition of Excision. Laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina—without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—performed on one or two sacral vertebral segments, as well as laminectomies extending over more than two cervical or thoracic vertebral segments, are listed as excluded methods. This distinction clarifies that Excision does not encompass these specific multilevel spinal procedures. Overall, Excision is portrayed as a versatile operative modality applied to cranial, orbital, breast, testicular, parathyroid, and facial regions, while being deliberately differentiated from extensive multisegmental spinal laminectomies.
